title: yeast
main_content: We analysed the genomic distribution of SVs and found it to be highly uneven, with significant enrichment in subtelomeric regions (two-sided Fisher exact test, P = 1.1 × 10−309). Although SNPs and indels are also enriched in these regions, the enrichment is much stronger for SVs (Fig. 2d). By computing structural diversity along the genome, we identified bursts of diversity that are often specific to a single SV type (Fig. 2e) and defined 46 SV hotspots (Supplementary Table5), including 21 translocation hotspots, nearly all in subtelomeric regions. One notable exception is a well-described reciprocal translocation between chromosomes 8 and 16, ... SSU1 gene located near the breakpoint38 (Fig. 2e). Hotspots for PAVs, CNVs and inversions often mapped to TE-rich regions or ... such as FLO39,40, CUP1 ..., and ... such as HPF1, SPA2 and NUM1 .... An inversion hotspot on chromosome 14 overlaps a 24-kb region flanked by inverted repeats, ...
context: ...which confers sulfite resistance through the overexpression of the SSU1 gene located near the breakpoint38...  SV-prone genes, such as FLO39,40 CUP1 (ref.41), ... genes with tandem repeats, ...(refs. 42,43) ... probably driven by recombination44
outcome: These findings indicate that SV hotspots arise either from genome fragility, linked to Ty elements or repetitive sequences, or from adaptive pressures targeting specific genes.
predicted_context: It is well established that subtelomeric regions in Saccharomyces cerevisiae are highly plastic and rapidly evolving, acting as dynamic hubs for structural variation. Furthermore, it is a known biological rule that genomic regions enriched with transposable elements (TEs) and inverted repeats are primary substrates for non-allelic homologous recombination (NAHR), which generates large-scale structural changes such as copy number variations (CNVs), translocations, and inversions. These dynamic regions often harbor expanded gene families involved in environmental adaptation and stress responses (such as FLO for flocculation and CUP1 for copper resistance), consistent with previous genomic surveys of yeast evolutionary dynamics.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that local genomic architecture—specifically the presence of subtelomeres, transposable elements, and repetitive sequences—creates localized zones of genomic instability that disproportionately drive structural variation. Consequently, structural variants (SVs) act as the primary evolutionary mechanism in these hotspots, enabling rapid expansion and diversification of gene families necessary for phenotypic adaptation, while keeping the broader chromosomal core relatively stable.

title: crispr
subsection: Scalable characterization of SpCas9 PAM variants
main_content: To generate datasets necessary to train a machine learning model to link protein sequence to function (PAM specificity),
context: structure–function-informed approach based on SpCas9 structures10 and previous engineering efforts3,14,42,43 ... These six  amino acids modulate specificity for the 3rd and 4th PAM bases in the context of the engineered enzymes SpCas9-VRER, SpCas9-VRQR and SpG3,14,42 (Supplementary Note3). ... the residue that contacts the second guanine of the SpCas9 PAM10, largely abrogated enzyme activity (Supplementary Note 3) ...
outcome: we sought to engineer and characterize a sufficiently large collection of novel SpCas9 enzymes with altered PAM preferences. We generated a saturation mutagenesis library of SpCas9 variant enzymes using a structure–function-informed approach based on SpCas9 structures10 and previous engineering efforts3,14,42,43, simultaneously mutating six amino acid residues within the SpCas9 PI domain,... We selected residues D1135, S1136, G1218, E1219, R1335 and T1337 for saturation mutagenesis, and individual enzymes within the library are hereafter named according to amino acid identities at these six positions. Expansion of our library to also mutate R1333, ... . We therefore focused our efforts on altering the 3rd and 4th positions of the PAM.
predicted_context: Training robust machine learning models requires large, quantitative sequence-to-function datasets. For CRISPR-Cas endonucleases, PAM specificity can be comprehensively profiled using high-throughput biological screens, such as bacterial PAM depletion or reporter-activation assays. In these systems, active Cas9 enzymes cleave plasmid libraries containing a fixed protospacer adjacent to a randomized PAM region (e.g., NNNN). The selective depletion or survival of plasmids is then quantified via next-generation sequencing, yielding a precise, data-rich landscape of recognized motifs. Previous studies have successfully utilized these massively parallel screening techniques to map the PAM requirements of both naturally occurring Cas9 orthologs and engineered variants, establishing it as a highly scalable method for generating comprehensive enzymatic functional profiles.
predicted_outcome: We will construct a library of engineered SpCas9 enzymes with combinatorially mutated PAM-interacting (PI) domains and subject them to a high-throughput PAM screening assay against a target plasmid library containing randomized PAM sequences, using next-generation sequencing to quantify the resulting PAM cleavage profiles for hundreds of these variants.

title: mechanical
subsection: Tumour gene expression altered by TME
main_content:
Interface cells downregulated MITF
and SOX10 and upregulated SOX9 (Fig. 1f and Extended Data Fig. 1g).
Classical melanocyte pigmentation genes were minimally expressed
by interface cells relative to the tumour bulk, including MITF (log2 fold
change (FC) = −5.19), TYRP1 (log2FC = −7.75) and PMEL (log2FC = −8.78)
(Fig. 1f, Extended Data Fig. 1g and Supplementary Table 1)... Unexpect-
edly, interface cells also upregulated genes involved in neuronal
development, including SOX11 (log2FC = 5.15), NNAT (log2FC = 8.61),
NEUROD1 (log2FC = 6.22) and NEFM (log2FC = 6.19) (Fig. 1g and Sup-
plementary Table 1). Gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) revealed that
transcriptional programs linked to cell fate specification and neuronal
development were highly enriched in interface cells (Fig. 1h,i).
context:
Melanoma behaviour is regulated by phenotype switching, in which
cells transition between differentiated/proliferative and undifferen-
tiated/invasive states. The transition between the proliferative and
invasive states is regulated by the transcription factors MITF, SOX9
and SOX10, among others8. MITF and SOX10 regulate melanocyte dif-
ferentiation and proliferation9,10, whereas SOX9 is associated with the
undifferentiated invasive state11.
outcome:
These
data indicate that interface cells adopt an invasive state with markers
of neuronal development.
predicted_context: It is well established that melanoma cells exhibit 'phenotype switching,' a form of plasticity where they transition from a proliferative, differentiated melanocytic state (driven by MITF and SOX10) to a dedifferentiated, invasive neural crest-like or mesenchymal state. This transition is frequently marked by the loss of MITF and the upregulation of alternative transcription factors like SOX9, which enables cells to adapt to microenvironmental stressors.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that mechanical confinement at the tumor-microenvironment interface acts as a spatial cue that directly drives transcriptional reprogramming, forcing melanoma cells to abandon their melanocytic identity and dedifferentiate into an invasive, neuronal-like progenitor state to facilitate tumor progression.

title: mechanical
main_content:
Tumour
cells invading into the TME showed elongated nuclei compared with
the bulk tumour mass (Fig. 1j,k).
context:
A previous study showed that tumour
nuclei become highly elongated when squeezing through mechanically
restrictive environments12. Although numerous factors probably influ-
ence tumour invasion,
outcome:
we hypothesized that mechanical forces exerted
on the cell/nucleus may cause stable changes in gene expression and
tumour cell behaviour.
predicted_context: Consistent with established principles of cell mechanobiology, it is well documented that nuclear deformation and elongation are direct morphological markers of cells subjected to spatial and mechanical confinement. Because the nucleus is the largest and stiffest cellular organelle, its elongation serves as a physical readout of the compressive forces exerted on cells as they navigate through dense extracellular matrices or tight interstitial spaces during 3D migration.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that the melanoma cells escaping the primary tumor are actively experiencing significant physical compression from the surrounding architecture, suggesting that mechanical confinement within the tumor microenvironment serves as the specific mechanotransductive cue that drives the phenotypic transition toward an invasive cellular state.

title: mechanical
main_content:
we hypothesized that mechanical forces exerted
on the cell/nucleus may cause stable changes in gene expression and
tumour cell behaviour. To test this hypothesis,
context:
a system to confine human mela-
noma cells (A375 cell line) in vitro at predefined heights (3 µm) using a
polydimethylsiloxane piston and micropatterned coverslips13
outcome:
we adapted a system to confine human mela-
noma cells (A375 cell line) in vitro at predefined heights (3 µm) using a
polydimethylsiloxane piston and micropatterned coverslips13 (Fig. 1l).
predicted_context: The cell nucleus acts as a mechanosensor; established biological principles dictate that spatial constraints and physical confinement (typically restricting cell height below the resting nuclear diameter) induce nuclear envelope deformation. This mechanical stress triggers downstream signaling that alters chromatin architecture, epigenetic states, and gene transcription. Previous studies investigating mechanotransduction and cellular plasticity have successfully modeled these forces in vitro by utilizing polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) microfluidic cell confiners to apply precise, uniform mechanical compression to cultured cells.
predicted_outcome: we will subject human melanoma cell lines to controlled mechanical compression using a PDMS microfluidic confinement device set to a severely restricted height (e.g., 3 µm) compared to unconfined controls. Following release from confinement, we will perform RNA-seq and ATAC-seq to identify stable transcriptomic and chromatin accessibility changes, and conduct 3D collagen invasion assays to evaluate the transition to an invasive phenotype.

title: mechanical
main_content:
These data indicate
that confinement causes interface cells to adopt a neuronal identity.
context:
Neuronal development relies on microtubule (MT) architecture,
influencing almost every aspect of neuronal structure and function14.
The cytoskeleton often transmits force within and between cells and
remodels in response to mechanical stimuli15. Recent reports indicate
that the MT cytoskeleton is stabilized by force to protect confined cells
from damage16.
outcome:
We hypothesized that melanoma cells hijack neuronal
mechanisms to allow them to invade into the mechanically confined
microenvironment.
predicted_context: In solid tumors and 3D microenvironments, mechanical confinement is frequently accompanied by dense extracellular matrix and poor vascular perfusion, leading to localized hypoxia. It is well established that hypoxia independently acts as a potent driver of phenotypic plasticity in melanoma, inducing dedifferentiation toward a neural crest-like or neuronal stem-cell state via HIF-1α-mediated epigenetic reprogramming and chromatin modifications.
predicted_outcome: Is the transition to a neuronal identity directly driven by mechanosensitive signaling and force-induced chromatin remodeling, or is it an indirect consequence of confinement-induced hypoxia, and can this phenotypic shift be prevented by maintaining strict normoxia or inhibiting HIF-1α in mechanically confined interface cells?

title: mechanical
main_content:
Confinement extensively
remodelled the MT cytoskeleton; within 2–4 h, curved MTs began encir-
cling the cell and nuclear periphery (Fig. 2a,b)...We also observed loss of a central
MT organizing centre with radial MTs,
context:
This was reminiscent of a previous study that showed that MTs bend to prevent buckling
or rupture in response to force16...reminiscent of neurons with
centrosome-independent MT organization14,17.
outcome:
These results indicate
that confined melanoma cells rapidly undergo structural changes in
the MT cytoskeleton resembling neurons.
predicted_context: It is well established that the transition from a centralized microtubule organizing center (MTOC) with a radial microtubule network to a reorganized, non-centrosomal array that encircles the nucleus is a characteristic cytoskeletal adaptation in cells undergoing 3D confined migration. This perinuclear microtubule remodeling is essential to absorb mechanical stress, protect the nuclear envelope from rupture, and facilitate amoeboid-like cell deformability in dense physical microenvironments.
predicted_outcome: Therefore, this indicates that mechanical confinement acts as a direct environmental cue that triggers rapid cytoskeletal reprogramming in melanoma cells, shifting them from a static, proliferative morphology into a mechanically resilient, invasive phenotype primed to navigate the physical constraints of the tumor microenvironment.

title: mechanical
main_content:
These results indicate
that confined melanoma cells rapidly undergo structural changes in
the MT cytoskeleton resembling neurons.
context:
Acetylated tubulin mediates neuronal architecture and function
and marks axonal MTs18. Tubulin acetylation stabilizes MTs and thus

outcome:
We performed immunofluorescence on sections from
adult fish with transgenic BRAFV600E-driven melanomas ... Confining A375 cells in vitro
predicted_context: To evaluate how spatial constraints dictate tumor cell morphology and intracellular organization, researchers must simulate mechanical confinement in vitro. Established models, such as polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) microfluidic devices or compressive agarose overlays, reliably subject cells to defined physical constraints that mimic the dense tumor microenvironment. Because the microtubule (MT) network dynamically restructures in response to such physical forces, observing its topology requires specialized imaging techniques. The established biological method is to couple in vitro confinement assays with high-resolution fluorescence microscopy, utilizing immunofluorescence targeted against primary structural components of the MT network (such as α-tubulin or β-tubulin) to accurately resolve cytoskeletal architecture and identify distinct morphological adaptations, such as neuron-like branching and neurite-like extensions.
predicted_outcome: Subject cultured melanoma cells to an in vitro mechanical confinement assay (e.g., using a microfluidic device or compressive hydrogel overlay), perform immunofluorescence staining specifically targeting α-tubulin, and utilize high-resolution confocal microscopy to image and quantify the morphological restructuring of the microtubule cytoskeleton into neuron-like networks.

title: mechanical
main_content:
However, most MTs in confined and
unconfined A375 cells were highly tyrosinated (Extended Data Fig. 4a–d), ...
Although confined perinuclear MTs were occasionally
tyrosinated and rarely detyrosinated (Extended Data Fig. 4a–d), there
was no specific enrichment of these PTMs in the perinuclear network,
as observed for acetylated MTs. Similar results were observed for poly-
glutamylated MTs (Extended Data Fig. 4e,f). No polyglycylated MTs
were found in either condition (Extended Data Fig. 4g,h).
context:
Detyrosination
has been linked to stabilized MTs26 ... probably owing to the high concentrations of tyrosine and tyrosinase in melanoma27
outcome:
This indicates
that acetylation is the primary PTM mediating the assembly and/or
stability of the perinuclear tubulin network.
predicted_context: It is well established that distinct microtubule (MT) post-translational modifications (PTMs) dictate the mechanical properties and functions of the cytoskeletal network. While tyrosination characterizes highly dynamic, recently assembled MTs, alpha-tubulin acetylation specifically marks long-lived, stable microtubules. Acetylation uniquely increases MT flexibility and confers mechanical resilience, protecting the filaments from breakage under severe mechanical stress and cellular deformation.
predicted_outcome: Therefore, the selective perinuclear enrichment of acetylated MTs—without a corresponding enrichment of dynamic (tyrosinated) or motor-regulating (polyglutamylated) PTMs—indicates that melanoma cells specifically reinforce the microtubule network surrounding the nucleus to provide structural stability and protect genomic integrity against the deformative forces of mechanical confinement.

predicted_context: The acetylation state of the microtubule network can be specifically manipulated without disrupting overall cytoskeletal polymerization by targeting regulatory enzymes such as the deacetylase HDAC6. Previous studies have established that Tubastatin A is a highly selective pharmacological inhibitor of HDAC6 that robustly induces α-tubulin hyperacetylation without altering global histone acetylation, thereby providing a precise method to isolate the functional role of the acetylated tubulin network in downstream signaling and gene transcription.
predicted_outcome: Treat human melanoma cells with the selective HDAC6 inhibitor Tubastatin A to pharmacologically induce microtubule hyperacetylation (and use α-TAT1 targeted siRNA for the inverse condition to deplete tubulin acetylation), then quantify HMGB2 protein and mRNA expression levels using Western blotting and RT-qPCR.

predicted_context: Microtubules can be pharmacologically stabilized without relying on endogenous post-translational modifications like alpha-tubulin acetylation. Paclitaxel (Taxol) is a well-characterized chemotherapeutic agent that binds directly to the beta-tubulin subunit on the inner surface of the microtubule polymer. This binding structurally suppresses microtubule dynamics and inhibits depolymerization, thereby forcing a stabilized microtubule network regardless of the cell's underlying acetylation state.
predicted_outcome: we treated the melanoma cells with the pharmacological microtubule-stabilizing agent paclitaxel (Taxol).

main_content:
Homer de novo motif analysis

P = 1 × 10−49) ...
Other highly ranked motifs included PRDM4, ... (Fig. 4k; P = 1 × 10−12), and SOX9, ... (Fig. 4k; P = 1 × 10−11).

outcome:
These data indicate that HMGB2 upregulation increases chromatin

predicted_context: It is well established that the transcription factors AP-1 and SOX9 are master regulators of the invasive, de-differentiated cell state in melanoma. They orchestrate transcriptomic reprogramming that drives phenotypic plasticity, converting melanoma cells from a proliferative state to a highly migratory, mesenchymal-like state. This is consistent with extensive epigenetic mapping studies that identify AP-1 as a central node in the gene regulatory network governing melanoma invasion.
predicted_outcome: Therefore, this indicates that HMGB2 acts as a critical upstream epigenetic modifier that drives phenotypic plasticity by selectively increasing chromatin accessibility at AP-1 and SOX9 binding sites. By remodeling the chromatin at these specific promoters, HMGB2 licenses these pro-invasive transcription factors to bind and activate the gene regulatory networks required for melanoma cells to transition into an invasive state in response to mechanical confinement.

To identify the specific genome-wide binding sites and downstream transcriptional targets of chromatin-associated proteins, Chromatin Immunoprecipitation followed by high-throughput sequencing (ChIP-seq) is the established and widely validated methodology used in prior studies mapping HMGB2 occupancy.
predicted_outcome: Perform Chromatin Immunoprecipitation followed by high-throughput sequencing (ChIP-seq) using an anti-HMGB2 antibody on melanoma cells to map the direct genomic loci and target genes bound by HMGB2.

title: mechanical
subsection: HMGB2 drives invasion through Notch and BRN2
main_content:
HMGB2 robustly
bound the promoter of Notch signalling genes in both A375 (Extended
Data Fig. 11d and Supplementary Table 6) and SKMEL5 (Extended Data
Fig. 12d and Supplementary Table 8) cells. Notch family genes were also
upregulated by human interface cells (DLL1, DLL3 and DLK2; Supple-
mentary Table 1) and confined human melanoma cells (NOTCH2NLA,
DLK2 and DLL4; Supplementary Table 2).
context:
Notch signalling promotes
melanoma invasion through brain-2 (BRN2, also known as POU3F2)
(ref. 48), a well-characterized pro-invasive transcription factor in mela-
noma49 that also functions in neuronal development50.
outcome:
This indicates that invasive and neuronal phenotypes in confined interface cells are
induced by HMGB2-mediated upregulation of Notch/BRN2 signal-
ling.
predicted_context: It is well established that the Notch signaling pathway is a critical driver of cellular plasticity, promoting the transition from a proliferative to a highly invasive phenotype in melanoma and other solid tumors. Furthermore, HMGB2 is a known architectural chromatin-binding protein that modifies chromatin accessibility to facilitate the transcriptional activation of target genes.
predicted_outcome: Therefore, we conclude that mechanical confinement acts as a microenvironmental cue that induces an invasive melanoma phenotype by driving HMGB2 to structurally remodel chromatin at Notch signaling promoters. This HMGB2-mediated chromatin reorganization upregulates Notch pathway components, serving as the mechanistic bridge between physical confinement and the transcriptional state governing melanoma invasiveness.

title: mechanical
main_content: Confined A375 cells downregulated many proliferation-related pathways. To visualize the influence of confinement on this trade-off,
context: FastFUCCI cell cycle sensor54
outcome:
we generated
an A375 cell line stably expressing the FastFUCCI cell cycle sensor54.
predicted_context: The phenotypic trade-off between proliferation and invasion in melanoma (often referred to as the 'go or grow' hypothesis) involves cell cycle arrest typically in the G1 phase. The Fluorescent Ubiquitination-based Cell Cycle Indicator (FUCCI) system is an established biological tool that leverages the spatiotemporal degradation of the cell cycle regulators Cdt1 and Geminin to visually distinguish cells in the G1 phase (red fluorescence) from those in the S/G2/M phases (green fluorescence). Previous studies have successfully utilized the FUCCI system in melanoma models to visualize this exact dichotomy, allowing researchers to track real-time transitions between proliferative and invasive states at the single-cell level.
predicted_outcome: we will transduce A375 melanoma cells with the FUCCI reporter system, subject them to mechanical confinement using a microfluidic confiner device (e.g., a PDMS ceiling compressing cells to a specific height), and perform continuous live-cell fluorescence confocal microscopy to quantify the spatiotemporal shift of the cell population from an actively proliferating state (green) to a G1-arrested, invasive state (red).

title: mechanical
subsection: HMGB2 is associated with drug tolerance
main_content:
Unconfined
cells rapidly displayed fragmented nuclear morphology and underwent
apoptosis, whereas confined cells were almost completely resistant
to Taxol-induced cell death (Extended Data Fig. 13d).
context:
Taxol induces
cancer cell death through cell cycle arrest60
outcome:
indicating that confine-
ment may cause melanoma cells to exit the cell cycle
predicted_context: Consistent with established literature, the phenotypic transition of melanoma cells from a highly proliferative state to a slow-cycling, invasive state is inherently coupled with non-genetic drug tolerance. Because chemotherapeutic agents like Taxol are anti-mitotic drugs that depend on active cell division to induce apoptosis, microenvironmental cues that shift cells away from active proliferation into an altered invasive state provide intrinsic resistance to such therapies.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that mechanical confinement by the tumor microenvironment acts as a critical biophysical switch that not only drives melanoma plasticity toward an invasive phenotype, but concurrently orchestrates a protective state that shields the cells from anti-mitotic chemotherapy.

Previous studies have demonstrated that isolating and sequencing non-hematopoietic (CD45-negative) bone marrow cells can successfully map the cellular taxonomy of the bone marrow stroma, revealing distinct phenotypic and functional shifts in mesenchymal, osteogenic, and endothelial populations driven by leukemic infiltration.

Deplete hematopoietic and immune cells (CD45+) using magnetic-activated cell sorting (MACS) or fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS), and perform single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) on the enriched non-immune stromal fractions.

main_content: This identified 13 cell surface signals common to both bcCML and AML, 18 unique to AML and 7 unique to bcCML. Of these 38 genes, 16 were misannotated as cell surface in the reference dataset19 and were removed from further analysis (Methods and Extended Data Fig. 2c,d). ... to identify ligands for these receptors,

predicted_context: Mapping intercellular communication networks between a tumor and its microenvironment requires identifying the upstream molecular partners of isolated receptors. A well-established bioinformatic approach to predict these interactions is to cross-reference identified target receptors with curated, literature-supported databases of known human ligand-receptor pairs (such as the FANTOM5 consortium dataset or CellPhoneDB). This allows researchers to reliably infer which corresponding ligands are produced by adjacent cell populations based on scRNA-seq expression data.
predicted_outcome: we queried the 22 remaining validated cell surface receptors against a curated ligand-receptor interaction database (e.g., FANTOM5) and subsequently analyzed the bone marrow tumor microenvironment (TME) scRNA-seq dataset to map which specific niche cell populations actively express their corresponding cognate ligands.

title: taurine
main_content: Consistent with a functional role of taurine from the TME in leukaemia progression, taurine levels were 1.7-fold higher in leukaemic bone marrow interstitial fluid as compared to the controls (Fig. 2j).
context: In mice, the majority of taurine is synthesized in the liver from cysteine24.
outcome: To determine whether taurine produced locally in the bone marrow niche is essential for leukaemia growth,
predicted_context: It is well established that the tumor microenvironment (TME) often undergoes metabolic reprogramming, resulting in the local accumulation of specific host-derived metabolites. Cancer cells exploit these nutrient-rich reservoirs by upregulating specific cell-surface transporters (such as SLC-family transporters) to fuel altered metabolic pathways, like glycolysis, and support rapid proliferation and survival under stress.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that the leukemic bone marrow niche actively secretes and enriches taurine to create a localized metabolic reservoir, which is subsequently hijacked by leukemia cells via the upregulated TAUT transporter to fuel glycolysis and drive leukaemogenesis.

title: taurine
main_content: These results suggest that, while TAUT loss does not impair steady-state haematopoiesis, it can impact long-term HSC self-renewal and maintenance.
context: These data are consistent with previous studies showing that genetic loss of LSC-enriched genes such as CD981, STAU26, MSI228,29, BRD430 and BCL231 can impair HSC self-renewal. However, therapeutic inhibition of CD98 showed minimal toxicity in AML phase I trials1 and BCL2 inhibitors are approved for AML therapy.
outcome: It is therefore possible that non-genetic approaches using small-molecule inhibitors or gene silencing may identify a therapeutic window for TAUT targeting in human cells.
predicted_context: It is well established that long-term hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) uniquely rely on stringent metabolic regulation, nutrient uptake, and stress-mitigating pathways (such as antioxidant defense) to prevent exhaustion during prolonged self-renewal, whereas committed progenitors and steady-state hematopoiesis are more metabolically flexible and resilient to the loss of specific metabolic pathways.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that taurine transport via TAUT represents a specialized metabolic dependency required specifically for preserving stemness and managing cellular stress over time. Consequently, targeting TAUT provides a therapeutic window to selectively disrupt the self-renewal mechanisms hijacked by leukemia stem cells (LSCs) without collapsing normal baseline blood production.

title: taurine
subsection: TAUT is essential for human AML growth
main_content: Our analyses of available gene expression datasets indicate that SLC6A6 expression is enriched in leukaemia stem/progenitors as compared to more mature blasts (Extended Data Fig. 8a). While SLC6A6 is broadly expressed in AML irrespective of karyotype or FAB subtype (TCGA; Extended Data Fig.8b), increased expression correlates with venetoclax resistance (BEAT-AML; Fig. 4a). SLC6A6 expression is also enriched in reactive oxygen species (ROS)-low LSCs from human monocytic AMLs (subtype clinically associated with venetoclax resistance; CD45bright SSChighCD117−CD11b+CD68+) compared with primitive AML (Fig. 4b; CD45medSSClowCD117+CD11b−CD68−)32. We find increased SLC6A6 expression in leukaemia cells carrying RAS mutations as compared to wild-type cells, ... (Fig. 4c). SLC6A6 is also highly expressed in relapsed AML originating from stem/progenitor like cells compared with more committed populations34 (Extended Data Fig. 8c).
context: ...consistent with data correlating RAS mutations with venetoclax resistance33
outcome: These data collectively indicate that inhibiting SLC6A6 may be of value across AML subtypes, including those commonly associated with venetoclax resistance.
predicted_context: It is well established that leukemia stem cells (LSCs) frequently maintain a low reactive oxygen species (ROS) metabolic state to preserve cellular quiescence, evade apoptosis, and survive traditional chemotherapy. Furthermore, distinct metabolic reprogramming and phenotypic shifts toward monocytic differentiation are known to be primary drivers of intrinsic and acquired resistance to the BCL-2 inhibitor venetoclax in acute myeloid leukemia.
predicted_outcome: Therefore, this suggests that the upregulation of the taurine transporter SLC6A6 is a critical metabolic dependency utilized by LSCs to manage oxidative stress and maintain their protective, ROS-low state. By exploiting SLC6A6-mediated taurine uptake, these primitive and monocytic AML populations acquire a distinct survival advantage that directly drives venetoclax resistance and disease relapse, positioning SLC6A6 as a highly actionable therapeutic target for overcoming therapy-resistant leukemia.

title: taurine
subsection: TAUT is essential for human AML growth
main_content: To determine whether small-molecule inhibitors of TAUT can effectively block leukaemia growth,
context: 6-aminomethyl3-methyl-4H-1,2,4-benzothiadiazine-1,1-dioxide hydrochloride35 (TAG) and guanidinoethyl sulphonate36 (GES;
outcome: we used two well-characterized structural analogues of taurine that inhibit uptake: 6-aminomethyl3-methyl-4H-1,2,4-benzothiadiazine-1,1-dioxide hydrochloride35 (TAG) and guanidinoethyl sulphonate36 (GES; Extended Data Fig.8d,e).
predicted_context: Pharmacological targeting of metabolic dependencies and nutrient transporters is a validated therapeutic strategy in oncology. Guanidinoethanesulfonate (GES) is a well-established competitive small-molecule inhibitor of the taurine transporter (TAUT/SLC6A6) that effectively restricts intracellular taurine uptake. Evaluating such targeted metabolic inhibitors in immunocompetent syngeneic murine models or patient-derived xenografts (PDXs) is the gold-standard preclinical approach to robustly assess in vivo therapeutic efficacy, impacts on leukaemia stem cell (LSC) function, and survival benefits within the physiological bone marrow microenvironment.
predicted_outcome: We will treat mice engrafted with aggressive myeloid leukaemia cells (such as a syngeneic MLL-AF9 murine model or human AML PDXs) with a small-molecule TAUT inhibitor, such as guanidinoethanesulfonate (GES). We will then monitor longitudinal leukaemia progression and overall survival using in vivo bioluminescence imaging, and quantify tumour burden and LSC populations in the bone marrow and spleen via flow cytometry compared to vehicle-treated controls.

title: taurine
main_content: Our untargeted metabolomic approach identified 13C label only in known taurine conjugates, N-acetyl taurine and glutaurine (Extended Data Fig.10f,g). Membrane-permeable taurine fully rescued colonies formed by Slc6a6−/− cells (Fig. 5g), and N-acetyl-taurine partly rescued this defect (Extended Data Fig.10h), ...  By contrast, glutaurine was unable to rescue the Slc6a6−/− colony formation (Extended Data Fig.10i).
context: perhaps by breakdown to taurine and acetate37.
outcome: These experiments indicate that taurine, and not a secondary metabolite, promotes leukaemia growth due to an indirect effect on glycolysis, possibly through downstream signalling.
predicted_context: It is well established that SLC6A6 encodes the primary cell-membrane taurine transporter (TAUT), which is essential for maintaining intracellular taurine pools. Once internalized, taurine is known to undergo limited biotransformation into specific downstream conjugates, such as N-acetyltaurine and glutaurine, to mediate physiological responses. In metabolic flux analysis, the functional dependency of a cell on a specific metabolic branch is conventionally validated by the differential capacity of these downstream metabolites to rescue growth defects induced by upstream transporter or enzyme ablation.
predicted_outcome: Therefore, this demonstrates that the loss of clonogenic capacity in SLC6A6-deficient leukaemia cells is a direct consequence of intracellular taurine depletion rather than an unrelated structural function of the transporter. Furthermore, it reveals a selective metabolic dependency, indicating that unconjugated taurine and its downstream conversion into N-acetyl taurine—but not glutaurine—are the functionally active metabolites driving leukaemogenesis and cell survival.

title: taurine
main_content: ... mTOR signalling has a key role downstream of taurine in leukaemia cells.
context: Amino acid availability can be sensed by RAG GTPases to facilitate mTORC1 localization to the outer lysosomal membrane40 and its interaction with RHEB GTPases41, thereby promoting mTORC1 activation.
outcome: We hypothesized that taurine may activate mTOR by promoting its interaction with RAG GTPases.
predicted_context: Cytotoxic T cells and other immune effector cells critically depend on mTOR-driven glycolysis for their activation, proliferation, and robust anti-tumor immune responses. Systemic inhibition of the mTOR pathway or its upstream metabolic activators can thus lead to profound immunosuppression and impair cancer immune surveillance.
predicted_outcome: Does therapeutic blockade of the taurine-mTOR-glycolysis axis inadvertently suppress CD8+ T cell effector functions within the bone marrow niche, and can the taurine transporter (TAUT/SLC6A6) be selectively targeted in leukemia cells without compromising systemic anti-tumor immunity?

(Extended Data Fig. 1a),

outcome: indicating high levels of degradation.
predicted_context: It is well established that RNA released into the bloodstream is immediately subjected to ubiquitous and highly active circulating ribonucleases (RNases). Consistent with multiple transcriptomic liquid biopsy studies, this continuous enzymatic cleavage rapidly degrades circulating transcripts into short fragments, completely obliterating the intact 18S and 28S ribosomal RNA (rRNA) species that are conventionally used as universal markers for cellular RNA integrity.
predicted_outcome: Therefore, traditional RNA quality control metrics, such as the RNA Integrity Number (RIN), are fundamentally inapplicable for assessing cell-free RNA. This indicates that to achieve ultra-sensitive ctRNA detection, assays like RARE-seq must be uniquely engineered to efficiently capture and sequence short, heavily degraded transcripts without relying on standard enrichment strategies that assume the presence of intact RNA molecules.

main_content: A germline deletion involving APOBEC3A and APOBEC3B (APOBEC3A/B), which effectively generates APOBEC3AAPOBEC3B fusion transcripts ... the deletion was not substantially enriched in patients with breast cancer compared with the background Korean population (51.0%, 844 out of 1,654 individuals, with 150 being homozygous and 694 heterozygous; P = 0.276 by two-sided chi-square test; Fig. 2g),
context: A germline deletion involving APOBEC3A and APOBEC3B (APOBEC3A/B), which effectively generates APOBEC3AAPOBEC3B fusion transcripts, has been suggested to predispose to APOBEC-mediated hypermutations29 (Extended Data Fig.4a).... Contrary to previous reports30,31,
outcome: suggesting that it is not a strong cancer-predisposing germline variant.
predicted_context: It is well established that the APOBEC3A/B germline deletion is highly prevalent in East Asian and Polynesian populations and has been widely investigated as a potential genetic susceptibility locus for breast cancer. Previous studies have demonstrated that this polymorphism contributes significantly to the burden of APOBEC-mediated somatic mutagenesis (a well-known mutational signature in breast tumors) and has been associated with increased breast cancer risk in certain cohorts.
predicted_outcome: Therefore, despite its prominent role in shaping mutational signatures within established tumors, the APOBEC3A/B deletion does not act as a strong independent driver or primary susceptibility allele for breast cancer incidence in the Korean population, suggesting its clinical impact is restricted to subsequent tumor evolution rather than initial oncogenesis.

title: breast_cancer
main_content: To determine the structural variation landscape,
context: A key mutational process in cancer is structural variation, which acts to amplify, delete or reorder chromosomal material at scales that range from single genes to entire chromosomes32
outcome: we generated a DNA interaction map between 5-Mb segments across the entire genome (Fig. 3a), along with large-scale copy number amplification patterns (CNAs; G-score).
predicted_context: Structural variations (SVs), including complex rearrangements, large deletions, tandem duplications, and translocations, are critical drivers of breast cancer genome evolution and instability. Previous comprehensive genomic analyses, such as the ICGC study of 560 breast cancers and the Pan-Cancer Analysis of Whole Genomes (PCAWG), have established that analyzing anomalous paired-end read mappings and split reads from high-coverage whole-genome sequencing (WGS) is the gold standard for comprehensively mapping these structural rearrangements in tumor-normal pairs.
predicted_outcome: we performed somatic structural variant calling on the matched tumor-normal whole-genome sequencing (WGS) data by identifying discordant paired-end and split reads using structural variation detection algorithms (e.g., Delly, Manta, or BRASS) to catalogue somatic translocations, inversions, deletions, and tandem duplications across the 1,364 breast cancer genomes.

title: breast_cancer
main_content: Another frequent interchromosomal translocation involved the ERBB2 locus on chromosome 17 and a locus on chromosome 20 (observed in 30 participants), ... (Fig. 3a, top right). ... structural variations involving the ERBB2 locus were clustered with super-enhancers genome-wide (P = 0.028 by permutation test; Fig. 3b), which translocated super-enhancers in about 90 kb from the ERBB2 locus (Fig. 3c). Tumours with extragenic structural variants in the vicinity (within 1 Mb) showed significantly higher ERBB2 gene expression levels than their gene copy number (log2 fold change >1, q-value <0.10; Fig. 3d). They often carried extragenic structural variants in the vicinity (within 1 Mb) with a cancer cell fraction (CCF) of approximately 1 (Fig. 3e), and distal breakpoints significantly enriched near breast cancer-specific super-enhancers35 (Fig. 3f).
context: where breast cancer super-enhancers were enriched35 ... Structural variations can influence  transcription by disrupting enhancer–promoter interactions, chromatin topology and long-range regulatory elements36. In line with this idea,
outcome: Our observation overall suggests a possible enhancer-hijacking mechanism for transcriptional activation in breast cancer genomes.
predicted_context: It is well established that somatic structural variations, such as translocations and inversions, can drive oncogenesis through a mechanism known as 'enhancer hijacking.' This process occurs when genomic rearrangements physically juxtapose highly active, distal regulatory elements (super-enhancers) into the spatial proximity of an oncogene, driving its aberrant, high-level transcription independently of traditional copy-number gene amplification.
predicted_outcome: Therefore, this indicates that enhancer hijacking serves as a distinct, non-canonical driver mechanism for ERBB2 activation in breast cancer. By structurally altering the genome to bring distal super-enhancers near the ERBB2 promoter, these clonal variants enable tumors to achieve pathological overexpression of the ERBB2 oncogene in the absence of canonical gene amplification.

title: breast_cancer
subsection: Clinical effect of ERBB2 focal gain
main_content: Out of 75 individuals with HER2-positive tumours who received neoadjuvant TCHP regimens (docetaxel (microtubule inhibitor), carboplatin (platinum intercalator), trastuzumab and pertuzumab (anti-HER2 inhibitors)), 38 showed pathologic complete response (pCR) (Fig. 4e and Supplementary Table 4). … cases with pCR (n = 38) exhibited significantly higher ERBB2 expression (P < 0.001, two-sided Student’s t-test) and a greater frequency of ERBB2 focal amplification (P = 0.001, two-sided Fisher’s exact test; Extended Data Fig.9f–h (external validation in the TransNEO cohort46)).  ... Notably, in our cohort, tumours with ERBB2  copy number of 33 or more were significantly more likely to be ecDNA-positive (P = 0.003; Fig. 4f, right); however, ERBB2 amplification by ecDNA was not associated with pCR rates (P = 0.540).
context: Of note, ecDNA is generally associated with poor prognosis across multiple cancer types47.
outcome: Our observation suggests that absolute copy number. rather than the amplification mechanism, may be a more relevant predictor of therapeutic response. It also indicates that ERBB2-amplifying ecDNA may be an actionable target for anti-HER2 therapy.
predicted_context: It is well established that extrachromosomal DNA (ecDNA) frequently drives massive oncogene amplification, promotes rapid intratumoral genetic heterogeneity, and is broadly associated with worse clinical outcomes, including resistance to targeted therapies across diverse cancer types.
predicted_outcome: Therefore, this suggests that while absolute ERBB2 expression and amplification levels are the primary drivers of response to dual HER2 blockade (TCHP), the topological architecture of this amplification (ecDNA versus chromosomal integration) does not uniquely confer therapy resistance or alter immediate neoadjuvant efficacy. This diverges from the general paradigm of ecDNA-driven treatment resistance, indicating that TCHP combinations can successfully overcome the high intratumoral heterogeneity typically associated with ecDNA in HER2-positive breast cancer.

title: breast_cancer
main_content: The timing analysis with the burden of co-amplified, pre-amplification somatic base substitutions yielded the acquisition timing of the frequent long-segmental CNAs in breast cancer in molecular time49 (Extended Data Fig. 10). Most of the recurrent long-segmental CNAs were acquired by 20% of molecular time, when recent common ancestral cells of a cancer emerge, which should be decades earlier than tumour diagnosis.
context: which is presumably acquired in early puberty, consistent with a previous report50
outcome: This implies that acquisition of the long-segmental CNAs is an early evolutionary event in breast cancer, which is presumably acquired in early puberty ... Our findings also suggest that full neoplastic transformation can take decades from the first event of genomic instability.
predicted_context: This observation is consistent with established models of tumor evolution, particularly the concept of punctuated equilibrium in cancer genomics. Prior evolutionary reconstructions, such as those by the Pan-Cancer Analysis of Whole Genomes (PCAWG) consortium and single-cell sequencing studies in breast cancer, have established that major chromosomal aberrations, including large copy number alterations (CNAs) and structural variations, often occur in early, massive bursts rather than through gradual accumulation, preceding clinical diagnosis by years or decades.
predicted_outcome: This indicates that the fundamental macroscopic genomic architecture of breast cancer is established by initiating structural instability events during the very early stages of tumorigenesis. Consequently, breast cancer development features a remarkably prolonged latency phase following this initial chromosomal disruption, providing a substantial temporal window where early detection strategies could theoretically target these primary, long-segmental clonal variations before extensive tumor outgrowth.
